metadata.summary: Quality standards for TypeScript, Python, React covering linting, type safety, testing, performance budgets, security. Configuration generator and audit driver; refresh quarterly.

content.title: Code Quality Trends 2026
content.summary: A snapshot checklist of current quality standards for mainstream stacks (TypeScript 5/React 19/Next.js 15, Python 3.12+, Go, Rust) covering linting, type safety, testing coverage targets, performance budgets, and security practices. Treat as a configuration generator and audit driver, not a source of truth — refresh quarterly.

why.p: Stack best practices shift faster than teams track them. An agent using this snapshot can generate stack-matched lint configs, CI workflows, and coverage gates for new repos in one pass, and score existing repos against a current baseline. Without an explicit checklist, agents fall back on training-data defaults that may be 1–2 versions stale.

list.item: Setting up a new repo: generate lint/format/test/CI configs matched to the detected stack.
Quarterly tech-debt audit: compare repo against checklist, emit prioritized gap list.
Onboarding a stack the team hasn't touched in 18+ months — surface what changed.
Defining CI performance budgets (FCP, API p95 latency, error rate) from the benchmarks table.
Critical stack decisions requiring judgment about team skill, hiring, or vendor lock-in — checklist is descriptive, not normative.
Late-stage products where a stack switch is prohibitively expensive — high adoption stats don't justify migration.
Niche stacks (Elixir, Clojure, Zig) — the checklist is mainstream-biased.
Real-time incident response — performance benchmarks are aspirational, not SLO replacements.

section.name: stack-checklist
section.p: Per-stack quality gates for TypeScript, Python, and React — used to generate configs and audit existing repos.

rule.statement: Enable strict mode in tsconfig.json; ban the any type (use unknown instead); add explicit return types on all public functions.
rule.rationale: TypeScript strict mode catches a class of runtime errors at compile time; any defeats the type system entirely.

list.item: strict: true in tsconfig.json
No any types — use unknown with type guards
Explicit return types on public functions
ESLint flat config + Prettier configured
tsc --noEmit in CI

rule.statement: Require type hints on all functions, mypy --strict enabled, ruff replacing black/isort/flake8, pytest with branch coverage >= 80%.
rule.rationale: ruff is 10-100x faster than the tools it replaces and enforces the same rule set; mypy strict catches annotation gaps that lead to runtime AttributeErrors.

list.item: Type hints on all functions
mypy --strict in CI
ruff for lint + format (replaces black, isort, flake8)
pytest --cov with branch=true, fail_under=80

rule.statement: Use Server Components by default; minimize client boundaries; verify accessibility with axe-core; enforce Lighthouse score > 90 in CI.
rule.rationale: React 19 Server Components reduce JS bundle size by default; early a11y checks are cheaper than retrofits.

list.item: TypeScript strict mode
Server Components where possible; minimize use client
axe-core a11y check in CI
Lighthouse CI score > 90
Performance budget: FCP < 1.8s, TTI < 3.8s
Input validation on all external inputs
No hardcoded secrets (gitleaks / trufflehog in CI)
SQL injection prevention (parameterized queries only)
Automated dependency security scans (pip-audit, npm audit)
CSRF tokens on state-mutating endpoints

antipattern.description: Treating security checklist items as binary tickboxes without threat modeling.
antipattern.reason: Real security requires understanding attack vectors, not just checking boxes; pair with security-review skill for sensitive changes.

list.item: Lint (ESLint flat config / ruff)
Type check (tsc --noEmit / mypy --strict)
Unit tests with branch coverage gate
Security scan
Build verification

section.name: tooling-and-benchmarks
section.p: CLI tool index, performance benchmarks, AI tool routing, and common agent failure modes for 2026 quality work.

rule.statement: Use these as CI budget targets, not SLOs; actual SLOs need geo-distributed measurement.
rule.rationale: Global averages mask regional regressions; p95 latency in SE Asia may be 5x the US number.

list.item: Frontend FCP: < 1.8s
Frontend TTI: < 3.8s
Lighthouse Score: > 90
Backend API p95: < 200ms
Database queries p95: < 50ms
Error rate: < 0.1%

rule.statement: Route by task type: daily inline suggestions to Copilot; large refactors and test generation to Claude Code; flow-state sessions to Cursor.
rule.rationale: Each tool has different context windows, latency, and agentic capability; routing by task type optimizes cost and quality.

rule.statement: Never auto-accept AI output for authentication code, data access logic, business rules, or security-sensitive areas.
rule.rationale: These areas have subtle correctness requirements that LLMs frequently miss; human review is mandatory.

antipattern.description: Stale-trend hallucination: agent cites benchmarks not in the doc (training data leaking in).
antipattern.reason: Use quote-only mode in prompt: "Use only the attached trend doc; do not cite from memory."

antipattern.description: Generated configs reference non-existent lint rules.
antipattern.reason: Always run the linter once on emit; auto-revert if it exits non-zero.

antipattern.description: Coverage gates without test infrastructure produce false greens.
antipattern.reason: Verify test count > 0 before enforcing a coverage threshold; --cov-fail-under=80 with zero tests still passes.

antipattern.description: Statistics parroting: agent quotes "81% of teams use AI" to justify a decision.
antipattern.reason: Filter statistical claims out of decision contexts; cite primary surveys when needed.

list.item: ruff — Python lint + format (replaces black, isort, flake8). Install: `pip install ruff`
mypy --strict — Python static types. Install: `pip install mypy`
eslint (flat config) — JS/TS lint. Install: `npm i -D eslint`
prettier — JS/TS/CSS/MD format. Install: `npm i -D prettier`
tsc --noEmit — TypeScript type-check in CI. Built-in to tsc.
pytest --cov — Python coverage. Install: `pip install pytest-cov`
vitest run --coverage — JS/TS coverage. Install: `npm i -D vitest`
lighthouse-ci — Lighthouse score gate in CI. Install: `npm i -g @lhci/cli`
k6 / wrk — Load testing for backend benchmarks. See https://k6.io
axe-core — a11y check matching the React-checklist row. Install: `npm i -g @axe-core/cli`
npm-check-updates / pip-tools — Dependency freshness. Install: `npm i -g npm-check-updates`
semgrep — Rule-based security scan. Install: `pip install semgrep`
gitleaks / trufflehog — Secret scan. Download from github releases.
GitHub Copilot — SaaS, IDE-native. "Daily coding" lane in routing table.
Cursor / Windsurf — SaaS, agent-friendly. "Flow-state" lane.
Claude Code — SaaS, agent-friendly primary. "Large refactor / test gen / review" lane.
Snyk / GitHub Advanced Security — SaaS, API-friendly. Dependency + SAST.
Renovate / Dependabot — SaaS/OSS, config-driven. Autonomous dep updates.
Sentry — SaaS, API-friendly. Backs the "<0.1% error rate" benchmark.
Datadog / Grafana Cloud — SaaS, API-friendly. p95 API latency monitoring.
Codecov / Coveralls — SaaS. Coverage gates.
Sonar / SonarCloud — SaaS/OSS, issues API. Maintainability rating.
Lighthouse CI Server — OSS, REST API. Self-hosted perf history.

section.name: agent-workflow
section.p: Use as a configuration generator + audit checklist, not a knowledge oracle. On a new repo: a setup agent reads package.json/pyproject.toml, infers stack, applies the matched checklist row, generates configs (eslint.config.js, ruff.toml, tsconfig.json strict, pre-commit hooks, GH Actions workflow). On an existing repo: an audit agent runs the checklist, scores compliance, produces a prioritized gap list. Re-run audit quarterly via cron and diff scores. Always validate generated configs by running them — agent generates plausible-but-broken configs at non-trivial rate.

list.item: stack-detector — reads manifest files and outputs {language, framework, version, build-tool}.
quality-config-gen — emits language/framework-matched configs from the checklist; sonnet.
quality-auditor — runs the checklist as boolean checks against the live repo, scores 0-100, lists gaps.
perf-budget-watch — Lighthouse/k6 runner per release; flags regressions vs the documented benchmarks.
dependency-freshness — non-LLM (npm outdated, pip list --outdated) + agent triage of breaking-change risks.
faion-improver (skill) — wraps the audit→fix loop as a session.

section.prompt: Audit this repo against the 2026 Code Quality Checklist for stack=<detected>. Output JSON array of {item, status:"pass|fail|na", evidence, fix_command_if_fail}. Cap items at 30; pick the highest-leverage from the matched stack list. Do not invent configs that don't exist; if a tool isn't installed, mark "fail" and propose installation.

Generate <eslint.config.js | ruff.toml | tsconfig.json | .pre-commit-config.yaml> for: stack=<...>, package_manager=<...>, target_node_or_python=<...>. Strict mode on. No deprecated rules. Reference the 2026 Code Quality Checklist for required items. Do not include rules that conflict with the project's existing Prettier/Black config. After emitting, output a one-line command to validate the config.

list.item: Use the trends doc as a checklist generator, not a source of truth. Cite primary sources (TC39, PEP, framework changelogs) in actual ADRs.
Pin tool versions in CI; "latest" creates non-reproducible audits.
Run quality audit as a cron-scheduled agent job, store score history. Trends matter more than single-point compliance.
Distinguish line/branch/mutation coverage in any "80%" gate. Mutation kill rate >75% is a meaningful target; line coverage is a vanity number.
Tie performance budgets to user-impacting metrics (FCP, LCP, INP) — not bundle size in isolation.
Always pair AI tool routing with kill-switches: every Copilot/Cursor/Claude Code change must pass the same lint/test/coverage gates as human-written.
Refresh the trends doc itself quarterly; agent-generated configs from a stale trends doc accumulate drift.

template.purpose: Coarse stack classifier: reads manifests, emits lang/framework/version for the audit agent.
Prompt for stack audit: output JSON array of {item, status, evidence, fix_command}.
